en.extract:
The chestnut antpitta (Grallaria blakei) is a species of bird in the family Grallariidae. It is endemic to Peru.


Taxonomy and systematics
The chestnut antpitta has always been regarded as monotypic. A study published in 2020 showed that two populations of it were actually separate species, the Oxapampa antpitta (G. centralis) and Ayacucho antpitta (G. ayacuchensis). By mid-2022 the International Ornithological Committee, the Clements taxonomy, and the South American Classification Committee of the American Ornithological Society had recognized the two new species, significantly reducing the range attributed to the chestnut antpitta. However, as of early 2024 BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World (HBW) had not recognized them.


Description
The chestnut antpitta is about 14.5 to 15 cm (5.7 to 5.9 in) long; six individuals weighed between 37 and 47 g (1.3 and 1.7 oz). The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have rufous brown upperparts. Their lores and most of their face are rufous. Their throat and breast are pale rufous, their belly whitish buff with obscure dusky barring, their flanks brown or olive brown, and their vent and undertail coverts tawny brown. They have a brown iris, a  black bill, and slate, blue-gray, or silvery gray legs and feet.


Distribution and habitat
The chestnut antpitta is found intermittently on the eastern slope of the Peruvian Andes from the Cordillera de Colán in Amazonas Department south for an unknown distance but short of northern Huánuco Department. It inhabits humid montane forest and secondary forest where it favors stands of bamboo and other dense undergrowth. 


Behavior


Movement
The chestnut antpitta is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.


Feeding
The chestnut antpitta's diet and foraging behavior have not been detailed; it is assumed to eat arthropods and perhaps small vertebrates as do other members of genus Grallaria . It is known to forage on or very near the ground.


Breeding
Nothing is known about the chestnut antpitta's breeding biology.


Vocalization
The chestnut antpitta's song is "a rapid, monotone, slightly accelerating series of chiming notes: chew'chu'uuuuuuuu'uuu".


Status
The IUCN follows HBW taxonomy and so its assessment of the chestnut antpitta includes the Oxapampa and Ayacucho antpittas. That species sensu lato is assessed as being of Least Concern. It is considered "locally fairly common".


References

The '''chestnut antpitta''' ('''''Grallaria blakei''''') is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Grallariidae]]. It is [[Endemism|endemic]] to [[Peru]].<ref name=IOC14.2>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/antthrushes/ |title=Antthrushes, antpittas, gnateaters, tapaculos, crescentchests |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 14.2 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=August 2024 |access-date=19 August 2024 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The chestnut antpitta has always been regarded as [[monotypic]].<ref name=IOC14.2/> A study published in 2020 showed that two populations of it were actually separate species, the [[Oxapampa antpitta]] (''G. centralis'') and [[Ayacucho antpitta]] (''G. ayacuchensis'').<ref name=Isler2020>{{Cite journal|last1=Isler|first1=Morton L.|last2=Chesser|first2=R. Terry|last3=Robbins|first3=Mark B.|last4=Cuervo|first4=Andrés M.|last5=Cadena|first5=Carlos Daniel|last6=Hosner|first6=Peter A.|date=2020-07-21|title=Taxonomic evaluation of the ''Grallaria rufula'' (Rufous Antpitta) complex (Aves: Passeriformes: Grallariidae) distinguishes sixteen species|url=https://www.biotaxa.org/Zootaxa/article/view/zootaxa.4817.1.1|journal=Zootaxa|language=en|volume=4817|issue=1|page=74 |doi=10.11646/zootaxa.4817.1.1|pmid=33055681|bibcode=2020Zoot.4817...74I |issn=1175-5334|url-access=subscription}}</ref> By mid-2022 the [[International Ornithological Committee]], the [[Clements taxonomy]], and the South American Classification Committee of the [[American Ornithological Society]] had recognized the two new species, significantly reducing the range attributed to the chestnut antpitta.<ref name=IOC11.2>Gill, F, D Donsker, and P Rasmussen (Eds). 2021. IOC World Bird List (v 11.2). Doi 10.14344/IOC.ML.11.2.  http://www.worldbirdnames.org/ retrieved July 14, 2021</ref><ref name=Clements2021>Clements, J. F., et al. 2021. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2021. Downloaded from https://www.birds.cornell.edu/clementschecklist/download/ retrieved August 30, 2021</ref><ref name=SACClist>Remsen, J. V., Jr., et al. Version 6 June 2022. A classification of the bird species of South America. American Ornithological Society. https://www.museum.lsu.edu/~Remsen/SACCBaseline.htm retrieved June 6, 2022</ref> However, as of early 2024 [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' (HBW) had not recognized them.<ref name=HBW8.1>HBW and BirdLife International (2024). Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. Version 8.1. Available at: https://datazone.birdlife.org/species/taxonomy retrieved August 26, 2024</ref>

==Description==

The chestnut antpitta is about {{convert|14.5|to|15|cm|in|sigfig=2|abbr=on}} long; six individuals weighed between {{convert|37|and|47|g|oz|sigfig=2|abbr=on}}. The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have rufous brown upperparts. Their [[Lore (anatomy)|lores]] and most of their face are rufous. Their throat and breast are pale rufous, their belly whitish buff with obscure dusky barring, their flanks brown or olive brown, and their vent and undertail coverts tawny brown. They have a brown iris, a  black bill, and slate, blue-gray, or silvery gray legs and feet.<ref name=CHAN-BOW>{{cite taxon |BOW |citation=Schulenberg, T. S. and G. M. Kirwan (2021). Chestnut Antpitta (Grallaria blakei), version 1.1. In Birds of the World (T. S. Schulenberg, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.cheant2.01.1 |access-date=September 16, 2024}}</ref><ref name=Schulenberg>Schulenberg, T.S., D.F. Stotz, D.F. Lane, J.P. O'Neill, and T.A. Parker III. 2010. ''Birds of Peru''. Revised and updated edition. Princeton University Press, Princeton, New Jersey. Plate 178</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==

The chestnut antpitta is found intermittently on the eastern slope of the Peruvian Andes from the Cordillera de Colán in [[Amazonas (Peruvian department)|Amazonas Department]] south for an unknown distance but short of northern [[Department of Huánuco|Huánuco Department]]. It inhabits humid [[montane forest]] and [[secondary forest]] where it favors stands of bamboo and other dense undergrowth.<ref name=CHAN-BOW/><ref name=Schulenberg/> 

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The chestnut antpitta is believed to be a year-round resident throughout its range.<ref name=CHAN-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The chestnut antpitta's diet and foraging behavior have not been detailed; it is assumed to eat [[arthropod]]s and perhaps small vertebrates as do other members of genus ''Grallaria ''. It is known to forage on or very near the ground.<ref name=CHAN-BOW/>

===Breeding===

Nothing is known about the chestnut antpitta's breeding biology.<ref name=CHAN-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Grallaria-blakei |species=the chestnut antpitta}}
===Vocalization===

The chestnut antpitta's song is "a rapid, monotone, slightly accelerating series of chiming notes: ''chew'chu'uuuuuuuu'uuu''".<ref name=Schulenberg/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] follows HBW taxonomy and so its assessment of the chestnut antpitta includes the Oxapampa and Ayacucho antpittas. That species ''[[sensu lato]]'' is assessed as being of Least Concern.<ref name=iucn>{{cite iucn |author=BirdLife International |year=2022 |title=''Grallaria blakei'' |article-number=e.T22703327A216684003 |doi=10.2305/IUCN.UK.2022-2.RLTS.T22703327A216684003.en}}</ref> It is considered "locally fairly common".<ref name=Schulenberg/>
